title: CNN-BiLSTM Based Channel Estimation in RIS-NOMA System for 5G and Beyond
authors: Aman Kanwar, Aryan Mittal, Akash, Sandeep Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 12th Uttar Pradesh Section International Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Engineering (UPCON)
abstract: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) is one of the key enabler along with Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) for beyond 5G (B5G) and 6G networks, offering enhanced spectral efficiency and massive connectivity. The estimation of RIS-NOMA channels is particularly challenging due to the cascaded nature of the BS-RIS-user transmission path combined with non-linear distortion introduced by NOMA-based signal superposition. In this study, a composite deep learning approach is designed that leverages convolutional neural processing for spatial feature learning and bidirectional recurrent memory structures to effectively characterize temporal correlations in the observed signals. A synthetic RIS-NOMA dataset is generated to capture user mobility, power imbalance, and varying RIS configurations. The model predicts the amplitude and phase of cascaded channels and is evaluated under multiple SNR levels using NRMSE and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> score. Simulation results show that the proposed CNN-BiLSTM model outperforms a baseline CNNLSTM, particularly in low-SNR and asymmetric power scenarios, demonstrating its potential for efficient channel estimation in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 G}$</tex> RIS-NOMA networks.

title: Bell Slotted Circular Patch Antenna for 5G and Beyond
authors: Anslin Star R, Anitha G
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: A new circular microstrip patch antenna featuring a unique bell-shaped slot specifically for millimeter wave usage. The antenna is fabricated on a Rogers RT/duroid 5870 substrate with circular patch radius of 0.933 mm. The design resonates at 59.75 GHz that achievs excellent impedance matching. A return loss of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-\mathbf{6 0. 5 ~ d B}$</tex> and a peak gain of 7.66 dBi is achieved when the bell-shaped slot is incorporated to the patch. A 1.4 GHz bandwidth is obtained which provides the reliable operation across the target spectrum. The bell slot geometry allows the antenna to remain compact without sacrificing performance making it an ideal fit for the next generation of 5 G and beyond wireless communication technologies.

title: Real-Time Traffic Sign Detection and Recognition for Smart Vehicles
authors: Zahraa Shihab Al Hakeem, Hiba J. Aleqabie
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Vision, Image and Signal Processing (ICVISP)
abstract: This paper proposes a real-time traffic sign detection and recognition framework tailored for smart vehicles in dynamic road environments. The system integrates convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for deep feature extraction with support vector machines (SVMs) for reliable classification. A robust preprocessing pipeline—comprising normalization, noise filtering, and color space transformation—ensures effective operation under variable lighting and weather conditions. Evaluated on a hybrid dataset of $\mathbf{1 8, 8 2 6}$ annotated traffic signs and real-time video frames, the system achieved an accuracy of $99.06 \%$, with a precision of $98.94 \%$ and a recall of $98.81 \%$. The false alarm rate remained low at $1.3 \%$, validating its practical reliability. Comparative assessments against state-of-the-art models such as YOLOv4-SPP and MobileViT demonstrate that the proposed system offers superior accuracy while maintaining real-time performance on modest hardware. Minor degradation was noted under conditions such as occlusion and low-light scenarios. Future enhancements will explore multi-frame tracking, sensor fusion (e.g., LiDAR and infrared), and lightweight model optimization through pruning and quantization. Overall, the proposed system provides an efficient, scalable, and high-performance solution for real-world deployment in intelligent transportation systems and autonomous driving platforms.

title: A Decentralized Cloud Computing Enhancement Framework Using Blockchain for Resource Allocation
authors: Ganji Ramanjaiah, Balajee Maram, Tummala Srinivasa Ravi Kiran, Komanduri Venkata Sesha Sai Ramakrishna
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Advancement in Futuristic Technologies (ICAFT)
abstract: We can use computing power as and when required from a common pool because of cloud technology. Even so, older clouds cannot work effectively. Leaving space underutilized increases the vulnerability of the hardware hub, in addition, they are not necessarily dispersed. A recent system uses block chain technology for increased trustworthiness, transparency, and autonomy of cloud resource sharing. A block chain refers to the digital ledger that is unchangeable and works offsetting on the cloud and other servers. A total of 50 computer network is recreated for running 1000 jobs. Paraphrase this (11 words). New configuration makes the resources work at 85% efficiency. The tasks are divided fairly among the participants who also contribute to the scoreboard. Everything is a mess but 98% is basically done for sure. Response times shrink versus older setups. Future networks refer to the 5G and beyond communications. Cloud services can prove beneficial in this area.

title: A SiC MOSFET Short-Circuit Protection And Fault Type Identification Circuit
authors: Qiyuan Zhang, Tailai Xiong, Xuan Lin, Taiqiang Cao
publish: 2025 4th Asian Conference on Frontiers of Power and Energy (ACFPE)
abstract: Silicon Carbide Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (SiC MOSFET) offers high power density but poor short-circuit withstand capability, so the design of fast and reliable short-circuit protection is crucial for the application of SiC MOSFET. Traditional short-circuit protection circuits based on di/dt detection suffer from weak noise immunity, poor detection flexibility, and high short-circuit current peaks during load faults, making it difficult to meet the practical short-circuit protection requirements for SiC MOSFET. Focusing on two types of short-circuit faults—Hard Switching Fault (HSF) and Fault Under Load (FUL), this paper proposes a fault identification circuit based on dv/dt and source current detection. The circuit can accomplish both short-circuit protection and fault type identification within $2~\mu$ s, providing important guidance for fault root cause analysis.

title: Emotion Detection from Facial Expressions Using Frame-Based Deep Networks
authors: Adalarasu B, R. A. Ayyapparajan, M. Umamaheswari, R. Prasanna, Sundaresh S
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Expressions are applied in personal health and social interactions, as expressions give information about the manner in which our emotional life is shared. There are different expressions that are developed in the human body, such as facial expressions, body expressions, vocal expressions, linguistic expressions, eye expressions, and behavioral expressions. Identifying expressions is the secret to success in both personal and business life. Knowledge of facial expressions is very important in the process of identifying the emotions of a person. Facial expressions are the most important aspect of the relationship of nonverbal communication that exists between individuals. The emotions are the point of focus of the concept, and the introduction of the concept of cognitive emotions AI, an AI system that is utilized for the recognition of the emotions present in the facial expressions of individuals in real time. In this case, the point of focus is only on some universal emotions, which include happiness, anger, sadness, love, surprise, fear, and indifference. There are different AI systems that are utilized in this process for the identification of the emotions that are recognized. The process of preprocessing, extracting the information, and the final prediction of the emotions of the subject occur as the system receives the input image.

title: Hybrid YOLOv8-VGG16-ResNet50 Architecture for Enhanced Multi-Class Rice Pest Detection on the IP102 Dataset
authors: Rajasekaran Arunachalam, J Mohana
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Rice pest infestation significantly reduces crop yield and poses a major challenge to sustainable agriculture. To address limitations in existing detection methods, this study proposes a hybrid YOLOv8-VGG16-ResNet50 architecture optimized using Adaptive Energy-based Harris Hawks Optimization (AE-HHO) for multi-class rice pest detection on the IP102 dataset. YOLOv8 is employed for accurate pest localization, while VGG16 and ResNet50 extract complementary texture and semantic features through an adaptive fusion strategy. Experimental evaluation demonstrates superior performance, achieving 97.2% classification accuracy, 94.6% F1-score, and 95.1% mAP@0.5, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches. Comparative analysis with a lightweight detector further highlights the effectiveness of the proposed framework. The results indicate that the proposed approach provides a robust and efficient solution for intelligent pest monitoring, contributing toward precision agriculture and sustainable crop management.

title: Enhancing Pre-Eclampsia Screening Through Machine Learning and Multi-Source Data Integration
authors: Sweety Ashok Wanave, Gauri M. Dhopavkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Trends and Innovations in ICT (ICEI)
abstract: Pre-eclampsia remains one of the most severe pregnancy complications, tormenting the maternal and neonatal outcomes all over the world. In order to mitigate this risk, clinicians need risk-stratification instruments that are not only rapid but also evidence-based. This paper presents a predictive framework in modules that would combine clinical, biochemical, and biosensor data into a single and clear risk estimate. It consists of five modules which are inter-relational as follows: (1) acquisition and harmonization of multi-source data, (2) preprocessing and feature construction, (3) supervised machine-learning modelling, (4) clinical risk stratification and decision support, and (5) independent validation and performance monitoring. The Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting (XG Boost) models are crossvalidated and hyper-optimized to ensure strong discrimination and calibration. Individual risk type categories are thus created and incorporated to the clinician dashboards effortlessly and the patients are given brief educational information. The proven system helps to detect high-risk pregnancies at early stages and implement timely interventions, including low-dose aspirin and enhanced monitoring. Demonstrating that machine learning can be directly introduced into obstetric practice, the framework shows that a data-driven approach can offer a practical mechanism to improve maternal safety and the initial diagnosis of pre-eclampsia through the synthesis of clinical records, laboratory data, and biosensor inputs.

title: Sugarcane Disease Detection Using Advanced Machine Learning and Image Processing Techniques
authors: Kishori Abhijeet Sawardekar Budhale, Bhaskar Vijayrao Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Trends and Innovations in ICT (ICEI)
abstract: Sugarcane is a vital crop in tropical and subtropical regions, with India among the largest producers. Its yield and quality are often reduced by diseases such as Red Rot, Smut, Wilt, Mosaic Virus, Rust, and Yellow Leaf Disease. Traditional detection methods are labor-intensive, subjective, and unsuitable for large-scale monitoring. This study presents a hybrid deep learning framework for automated sugarcane disease detection using image processing techniques. Highresolution leaf images were collected from field surveys and online repositories, then preprocessed with Adaptive Histogram Equalization and k-means clustering to enhance segmentation and contrast. Statistical features were extracted using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). A convolutional neural network (CNN), enhanced with transfer learning, served as the primary classifier, while a Support Vector Machine (SVM) provided secondary validation. Experimental results show that the hybrid CNN-SVM model achieved strong performance across multiple disease categories, with Red Rot detection particularly accurate. Comparative evaluation confirmed that the hybrid approach outperformed CNN-only models, demonstrating the complementary strengths of deep learning feature extraction and SVM decision boundaries. Integration into mobile-based platforms enabled real-time diagnostic feedback, reducing delays and supporting timely farmer interventions. Future directions include expanding datasets, incorporating climatic variables, and applying Explainable AI (XAI) and multimodal learning frameworks to improve transparency, scalability, and generalization. This research establishes a foundation for intelligent, field-ready disease detection systems that advance precision agriculture and promote sustainable sugarcane production.

title: Consensus for linear multi-agent systems with robustly handle uncertainties in dynamic event-driven framework
authors: Yihang Weng, Xingzhan Liao, Gengxin Chen, Tingting Huang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: This paper presents a novel approach to robustly handle the linear multi-agent systems’ parameter uncertainties within a dynamic event-driven framework, ensuring consensus is maintained even under varying conditions. Uncertainties are paradigm bounded, embodied in dynamically changing state models, exacerbating complexity. The proposed method leverages an event-driven control strategy that reacts to state changes rather than operating on a fixed time schedule, where the state changes are dependent on set triggers being met. The triggers, characterized by dynamic variability in conjunction with the comparative state of adjacent agents at event spans, consistently maintain a value greater than 0 to effectively preclude any occurrence of Zeno behavior. By rigorous proof, it is shown that a solution necessarily exists and a method for solving it is given. The final arithmetic example proves the validity of the conclusion.

title: Adaptive Split Federated Learning for Network-Driven Cut Selection in Vehicular Edge Intelligence
authors: Youngjoon Yang, Sunghyun Kim, Chaehyeon Kim, Dongkyun Kim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Information and Communication (ICAIIC)
abstract: Vehicular Edge Intelligence (VEI) enables Roadside Units (RSUs) to locally process roadway images, train objectrecognition models, and trigger collision-warning functions. RSUs placed in different road environments observe different scene distributions, which leads to Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) local datasets. As a result, each RSU tends to overfit frequently observed classes while learning limited representations for rare but safety-critical objects, and this imbalance makes global convergence in distributed training unstable. To overcome the limitations of pure local learning, RSU-server collaborative training has been investigated as a more suitable approach, and Split Federated Learning (SFL) provides a practical architecture by combining split execution with parameter aggregation. However, vehicular communication channels vary over time in terms of bandwidth, distance, and interference, so a fixed cut layer in SFL cannot balance activation transmission cost and clientside feature expressiveness across RSUs. This paper proposes a channel-driven Adaptive Split Federated Learning (ASFL) scheme that selects the split layer dynamically according to real-time RSU-server link quality and available bandwidth. RSUs with weak channels are assigned shallower cuts, whereas RSUs with favorable links use deeper cuts to exploit richer feature extraction. Comparative experiments under different adaptive cut-selection configurations demonstrate that the proposed ASFL framework achieves improved model accuracy and reduced training latency by effectively balancing computation and communication overheads. These results indicate that network-aware adaptive partitioning is a promising strategy for distributed learning in VEI settings.

title: Integrating Data Science and Meerkat Clan Optimization for Predictive Cloud Task Scheduling
authors: Devanga Dharani Lakshmi, Raveendrababu Vempati, Maheswari Bandi, A Chandrakala, C Veena, Santhosh Kumar Medishetti
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: An intelligent predictive task scheduling framework for cloud computing is presented that integrates data science techniques with the Meerkat Clan Algorithm (MCA) to enhance scheduling efficiency, reliability, and scalability. The framework employs predictive modelling to estimate task execution time, virtual machine (VM) failure probability, and expected throughput, enabling proactive and informed scheduling decisions in dynamic cloud environments. These predictive insights are incorporated into an MCA-based multiobjective optimization strategy that simultaneously minimizes makespan and VM failure rate while maximizing throughput. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is evaluated using realistic Alibaba GPU workload traces and implemented within the SimPy discrete-event simulation environment to accurately capture cloud system dynamics. Comparative experimental results against Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), and Enterprise Development Optimizer Algorithm (EEOA) demonstrate substantial performance improvements. On average, the proposed scheduler achieves makespan reductions of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 5. 4 \%}$</tex>, lowers VM failure rate by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 1. 5 \%}$</tex>, and improves throughput by 34.4 % across varying workload scales. These results confirm that combining predictive analytics with clanbased meta-heuristic optimization provides a robust and scalable solution for next-generation, data-intensive cloud computing task scheduling.

title: TMF-Net: A Transformer-Based Multi-Modal Fusion Framework Integrating MRI and Clinical Data for Robust Brain Tumor Detection
authors: Sri Lakshmi M, Deepthi Kothapeta, Vijaya Kumar Koppula
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Diagnosis of brain tumors is a critical clinical exercise, and the selective differentiation between the benign tumors and the malignant ones is inseparable in the maximization of treatment regimens. In Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), there is a lot of space and structural information as relates to tumor morphology; however, the imaging data alone might not be sufficient to cover patient-specific biological variables that alter tumor dynamics. In this framework, we present TMF-Net, a Transformer based multimodal fusion model which integrates MRI imaging to structured clinical genetic data during brain tumor classification. The given architecture makes use of modality-specific Transformer encoders to extract salient representation of both MRI and clinical modalities, and then combines them through cross-attention fusion mechanism that captures inter-modal relationships. Full analyses of the BraTS MRI dataset with and without clinical glioma grading data show that TMF-Net significantly outperforms both unimodal baselines and traditional fusion methods with an overall classification accuracy of 98.8%. In addition, Grad-CAM visualizations provide interpretability through outlining clinically relevant tumor areas, thus supporting the accuracy of the framework in clinical decision support implementation.

title: Research on the Prediction Algorithm of Fusion Feature of Breast Cancer Mri Molecular Typing Based on Deep Learning
authors: Li Xu, Liang Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power, Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Control (PEEEC)
abstract: Accurate molecular typing of breast cancer is the key to formulating effective treatment plans and improving cure rates. This study proposes a multi-scale attention fusion CNN algorithm to fuse breast cancer MRI multi-sequence features to achieve molecular typing prediction. The experiment used MRI image data of 312 breast cancer patients, which were divided into training set, validation set and test set according to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7: 1: 2$</tex> after preprocessing. The experimental simulation results show that the prediction accuracy of the algorithm for luminal A type, luminal B type, HER2 overexpression type and triple negative breast cancer reached 85.6 %, the precision rate was 84.2 %, the recall rate was 83.8 %, and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> value was 84.0 %. Compared with the support vector machine (SVM), the accuracy rate was increased by 12.3 %, compared with the ordinary CNN algorithm and the CNN algorithm based on a single sequence feature, it has obvious advantages in all indicators. The algorithm provides efficient and accurate technical support for the early diagnosis of breast cancer and has important clinical application value.

title: Hate Speech Detection in Romanized Sinhala Text Using Multilingual Transformer Models
authors: Sakun Chamikara, Deshan Sumanathilaka
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
abstract: Social media has become a key platform for communication, but it has also amplified the spread of hate speech, particularly in languages and dialects that lack digital resources and support. One such case is Romanized Sinhala, an informal writing style commonly used by Sri Lankans. Its irregular spelling, use of phonetic forms, and mixing with English present serious challenges for automatic hate speech detection. This project explores a deep learning-based solution to detect hate speech written in Romanized Sinhala, with the aim of strengthening online safety in low resource language settings. To build the system, the SOLD (Sinhala Offensive Language Dataset) was transliterated into Romanized Sinhala using a custom pipeline. Several state-of-the-art multilingual transformer models, including XLM-RoBERTa, IndicBERT, and distil-mBERT, were evaluated to identify the most effective approach. Among them, multilingual BERT (mBERT) achieved the best performance, with an accuracy of 81.28 %, F1-score of 0.78, and recall of 0.81 after fine-tuning on the Romanized dataset. These findings highlight that pre-trained multilingual models, when adapted appropriately, can be effective even for languages with limited digital infrastructure. Beyond technical outcomes, this project has broader social relevance. It provides a foundation for developing tools that can reduce harmful content in Sinhala-speaking online communities, support moderation efforts, and promote healthier digital spaces in Sri Lanka.

title: Short-Horizon Load Forecasting for Peak-Shaving Opportunities
authors: Niño Louie R. Boloron, Louine Jacob P. Butalid, Arman T. Gascon, Chyre Jane C. Camaso, Cleifford S. Alfarero
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Power Engineering (AIPE)
abstract: We develop a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5}$</tex> - and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 0}$</tex>-minute persistence-anchored forecasting pipeline with train-only percentile triggers for peak capping in an anonymized executive government office. The dataset spans roughly three weeks of 15-minute active power measurements with limited features, reflecting realistic constraints in public buildings. Using expanding walk-forward validation, persistence is competitive with and often more accurate than ridge regression and gradient boosting for this short, feature-poor series. Peak flags are derived from train-only percentiles (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$85 \text{th} / 90 \text{th} / 95 \text{th}$</tex>) of active power and translated into actions via an inventory-bounded curtailment budget that assumes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 5 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 \%}$</tex> deployable shares of climate and lighting loads. Under leakage-safe evaluation and pragmatic data cleaning, the pipeline reduces monthly maxima when flags coincide with peaks and yields kWh savings across flagged intervals while bounding operator burden (actions per month) and false-action rates. We interpret persistence as sufficient under current data and infrastructure constraints, while acknowledging that richer datasets and exogenous variables can justify more complex models. The method is building-management-system (BMS) friendly, reproducible, and aligned with SDG 7 by improving operational energy efficiency under realistic conditions.

title: Harnessing Technology for SME Expansion and Market Reach: Evidence from Bahrain
authors: Waleed A. Aziz
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Sustainable Islamic Business and Finance (SIBF)
abstract: This study examines Bahrain Development Bank's (BDB) innovation-driven digital transformation through the development of the Tijara Online Banking Platform. Designed to enhance SME financing in Bahrain, Tijara emerged as a strategic response to unmet market needs, accelerated by the COVID-19 crisis. Using qualitative methods, including interviews with key managers and thematic analysis of internal reports, the research explores the platform's journey from inception to implementation. Tijara's development was guided by agile project management, cross-functional collaboration, and a hybrid model combining internal and external resources. Leadership alignment, staff training, and cultural transformation were critical to success. The platform achieved strong adoption, improved loan processing, and earned external recognition. However, challenges remain, including SME digital literacy, system limitations, and regulatory adaptation. Recommendations include forming a dedicated innovation unit, enhancing stakeholder engagement, and integrating AI and open banking tools. The study suggests scaling Tijara across the GCC through regional partnerships and whitelabeling strategies.

title: Comparative Analysis of Benchmarks in Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Anderson G. Araujo, Lucas B. B. Santana, Edward D. Moreno
publish: 2026 IEEE 17th Latin America Symposium on Circuits and System (LASCAS)
abstract: This article presents a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) on benchmarks in Machine Learning and Deep Learning, analyzing their characteristics, applications, limitations, and challenges. The methodology followed Kitchenham's guidelines, and five research questions (RQs) were formulated to guide the identification and analysis of studies. These RQs focused on the most used benchmarks, their influence on model generalization and performance, practical purpose, categorization criteria, and employed frameworks. The search was conducted across databases such as Scopus, IEEE Xplore, Web of Science, and ACM Digital Library. This process resulted in the selection of 76 articles, 12 of which were analyzed in depth. The results detail specific benchmarks for embedded systems (EEMBC, MiBench), mobile devices (3DMark, AnTuTu), and AI (AIBench, MLPerf). The findings highlight their importance in hardware and software evaluation, bottleneck identification, and reproducibility promotion.

title: GNN-Based Multi-Agent RL for Dynamic AP-CPU Assignment in Cell-Free Massive MIMO
authors: Mahnoor Ajmal, Joohwan Park, Dongkyun Kim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Information and Communication (ICAIIC)
abstract: The deployment of cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) systems at scale requires distributed processing across multiple central processing units (CPUs). The assignment of access points (APs) to CPUs directly impacts computational load distribution and network performance. Existing optimization methods rely on global channel knowledge and iterative solvers, limiting real-time applicability. This paper presents a graph neural network (GNN) framework with centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE) for AP-CPU assignment. The network topology is represented as a graph where APs form nodes and edges capture interference relationships. Graph attention layers generate embeddings that enable each AP to select its CPU assignment through a sequential coordination mechanism. Simulation results for a network with 50 APs and 20 users demonstrate 78% reduction in CPU load imbalance compared to distance-based assignment, while maintaining equivalent throughput. The learned policy achieves the highest minimum user rate of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10.82 \text{bps} / \text{Hz}$</tex> among all methods. Additional scalability experiments across 30 to 70 APs confirm consistent improvements of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 3}-\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex>, validating the effectiveness of learning-based approaches for backhaul resource management.

title: Transformer-Based Single-Channel EEG Modeling for Mental State Classification in Wearable BCIs
authors: Ravichander Janapati, Usha Desai, Sanjeevakumar M. Hatture, Viswaprakash Babu, Rashmi P. Karchi
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Mental state recognition with single-channel electroencephalogram (EEG)-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) plays significance in lightweight and wearable devices. However, a majority of traditional deep learning models are based on convolutional or recurrent networks that do not inherently feature long-range temporal and global attention patterns of an EEG signal. This paper provides a transformerbased single-channel EEG modeling model to classify the mental state in wearable BCIs. The proposed approach utilizes a temporal self-attention process to acquire contextual connections in whole sequences of EEGs, which allows better capturing of cognitive and emotional differences. EEG signals from the MetaWave Single-Channel EEG Mental State Dataset were first filtered with bandpass <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.5-40 \text{Hz})$</tex> and notch <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(50 \text{Hz})$</tex> filters to eliminate physiological and environmental noise. Short-time Fourier Transform (STFT) characteristics were obtained and converted to time-frequency representations as an input to transformer. This model was applied for inter-sequence correlations using a multi-head self-attention encoder, to predict mental states, including focus, relaxation, and stress, using a feed-forward classifier. The experimental findings show that the proposed transformer model attained 94.7% classification accuracy and an area under curve (AUC) of 0.97, which effective than the state-of-the-art models. The results confirm the efficiency of attention-driven temporal modeling towards a costeffective and efficient EEG decoding, which offers a pathway to the third-generation portable BCIs that will be able to assess mental state in real-time scenarios at low costs in daily setups.

title: From PBFT to Blockchain: A Comprehensive Survey of Byzantine Fault-Tolerant Consensus
authors: Akaash Vishal Hazarika, Joshit Mohanty
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) has developed from a theoretical concept in distributed database reliability to the basic structure of today's decentralized finance and global infrastructure. This paper provides a holistic overview of BFT approach, delineating imperative strides from classical synchronous resolutions to the most recent blockchain protocols. We give a formal treatment of the transition of the architecture from quadratic complexity (O(n2)) in PBFT to linear scalability <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(O(n))$</tex> in HotStuff and probabilistic guarantees, as compared with Nakamoto Consensus. Moreover, we present a critical review of some recent developments in 2024 and 2025 related to Machine Learning-accelerated adaptive consensus, probabilistic relaxation for high volume trading, lightweight protocols for IoT. By combining theoretical limits with a range of practical issues such as state transfer and cryptographic overhead, we hope that the survey provides a structured roadmap for researchers tackling the scalability-security trade-offs in future distributed systems.

title: Data-Level Parameter Sampling for Channel-Adaptive Semantic Communications
authors: Chan Hyung Kim, Geonhui Lee, Minju Chae, Seung-Chan Lim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Information and Communication (ICAIIC)
abstract: Although semantic communication leverages deep learning-based joint source-channel coding to achieve robust transmission, transceiver models trained at a fixed signal-tonoise ratio (SNR)often fail to generalize under varying channel conditions. To address this limitation, we propose a data-level parameter sampling (DLPS)-based training framework. The proposed framework employs an equivalent parallel additive white Gaussian noise channel model to enable stochastic sampling of channel parameters during end-to-end training. In the proposed DLPS strategy, a single SNR value is assigned to each training data sample, providing structured exposure to diverse channel conditions and improving channel adaptability. The simulation results show that the proposed framework maintains strong reconstruction performance across a wide SNR range, thus outperforming alternative sampling strategies and achieving competitive performance with SNR-specific dedicated training using a single unified model.

title: An Algorithmic Approach to Optimizing Network Routing Using Dijkstra's Algorithm
authors: Siva Sibi M, Hepzibah Christinal A, Anitha J
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: The high speed, increasing complexity of the modern computer networks have set up a dire requirement of the effective and dependable route systems to ensure the optimum levels of data flow. Conventional types of routing are usually unable to react to dynamically changing conditions of the network like congestion, fluctuating bandwidth capabilities, and node failures. The current paper proposes an algorithm- based approach to network routing optimization using binary variation of Dijkstra Algorithm which is a method that is dynamically calculate shortest and most stable path between nodes in real time. Three types of network parameters proposed, e.g., traffic load, link reliability, and latency, are implemented into the path- cost calculation in the proposed model which increases the adaptability in dynamic environments. To see the efficiency of the proposed approach with respect to the traditional shortest path routing algorithms, a simulated network topology was developed. Through experimental results, it is shown that the average data latency and the packet loss rate have been considerably reduced and throughput efficiency is increased. The results prove that the modified Dijkstra based algorithm has been an efficient and scalable computationally approach that is applicable to the routing of large scale and adaptive routing in the next generation network infrastructures.

title: Empowering Women in the Digital Economy: Mobile Wallets, Digital Cards, and the Moderating Role of Digital Financial Literacy
authors: Amanda Perera, Neranjana Ekanayake, Kaushalya Silva
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
abstract: The rapid expansion of digital payment systems has created new opportunities for advancing financial inclusion, particularly among working women in urban areas. This study examines the impact of mobile wallet usage and digital card usage on the financial inclusion of working women in the Colombo District of Sri Lanka, while investigating the moderating role of digital financial literacy. A quantitative research design was employed using a structured online questionnaire administered to employed women residing in the Colombo District. Data were collected through professional email networks and widely used social media platforms, with screening criteria ensuring that respondents were active users of digital payment tools. After data screening and cleaning, 389 valid responses were retained for analysis. The results show that both mobile wallet usage and digital card usage have a significant positive effect on financial inclusion by improving access to formal financial services and supporting convenient transactions. Digital financial literacy significantly moderates the relationship between mobile wallet usage and financial inclusion, indicating that higher digital competence strengthens the effectiveness of mobile-based financial tools. However, no significant moderating effect was observed between digital financial literacy and digital card usage, suggesting that cardbased payment systems are less sensitive to differences in literacy levels among urban working women. This study contributes to the digital finance and financial inclusion literature by empirically demonstrating the combined and moderating effects of digital payment usage and digital financial literacy within an urban, developing-country context. The findings offer policy-relevant insights for financial institutions, regulators, and technology providers seeking to enhance the financial inclusion of working women in Sri Lanka. These insights support digital finance initiatives for working women.

title: Enhancing Energy Efficiency in STAR-RIS-Assisted Massive MIMO-RSMA Networks
authors: Ridho Hendra Yoga Perdana, Thong-Nhat Tran, Wonseok Lee, Young Jeon, Taejoon Kim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Information and Communication (ICAIIC)
abstract: This paper investigates energy-efficiency maximization (EEM) for downlink simultaneously transmitting and reflecting (STAR-RIS)–assisted massive MIMO (mMIMO) networks employing rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA). The energy efficiency formulation problem is a non-convex mixed-integer program, driven by (i) an upper bound on the allocable common SE, (ii) the transmit-power budget at the base station (BS), and (iii) the discrete phase shifts at the STAR-RIS, which together preclude direct optimal solutions. To address this, we first relax the problem and decouple it into two subproblems—phase-shift design and beamforming—that are solved in an alternating fashion. The phase-shift subproblem is handled via a bisection search, while the beamforming subproblem is tackled using an inner-approximation (IA) procedure that converts the original non-convex constraints into tractable surrogates. For real-time operation, we design a deep-learning (DL) framework that infers optimal phase shifts and precoding matrices under varying system parameters. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme yields notable EE gains, and we further quantify the impact of the number of STAR-RIS and STAR-RIS elements on the overall system performance.

title: Towards Deploying VLA Without Fine-Tuning: Plug-and-Play Inference-Time VLA Policy Steering Via Embodied Evolutionary Diffusion_supp1-3678455.mp4
authors: Zhuo Li
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant potential in real-world robotic manipulation. However, pre-trained VLA policies still suffer from substantial performance degradation during downstream deployment. Although fine-tuning can mitigate this issue, its reliance on costly demonstration collection and intensive computation makes it impractical in real-world settings. In this work, we introduce VLA-Pilot, a plug-and-play inference-time policy steering method for zero-shot deployment of pre-trained VLA without any additional fine-tuning or data collection. We evaluate VLA-Pilot on both simulation and real-world experiments across distinct robotic embodiments. Experimental results demonstrate that VLA-Pilot substantially boosts the success rates of off-the-shelf pre-trained VLA policies, enabling robust zero-shot generalization to diverse downstream tasks and embodiments. Experimental videos and code are available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://rip4kobe.github.io/vla-pilot/</uri>.

title: Noncommutative Quantum Dynamics for Feature Representation Learning
authors: Hirokazu Shimauchi
abstract: We propose an explicit feature representation for supervised learning derived from short quantum dynamical trajectories composed of generally noncommuting operations, conceptually inspired by reservoir computing in that fixed, untrained dynamics are coupled to a simple trained readout. For each input, a fixed-depth circuit generates a sequence of prefix states, and features are formed by concatenating expectation values of a predetermined set of single- and two-qubit Pauli observables recorded across depths; multiple randomized but fixed circuit instantiations are aggregated to improve stability. A classical l2- regularized logistic-regression readout is trained on the resulting explicit features. To isolate representational effects, all methods are evaluated under identical preprocessing, repeated stratified holdout splits, and a unified model-selection protocol on a controlled subset of 11 tabular classification datasets, and are compared against classical baselines, including random Fourier features and second-degree polynomial expansions, as well as reservoir-style quantum baselines, with feature dimensionality matched when feasible. We further examine controlled variants targeting commutativity, entangling structure, history concatenation, and observable choice. A staged evaluation contrasts an idealized exact-expectation regime with finite-shot estimation under fixed and larger measurement budgets, thereby assessing sensitivity to sampling noise after re-tuning regularization. Beyond predictive performance, we analyze the induced geometry via empirical kernel spectra and effective-dimension diagnostics to contextualize the representations from a regularization-aware capacity perspective. Empirically, the strongest support concerns noncommutativity and history concatenation, whereas the contribution of the entangling block is mixed; the trajectory-based features induce empirically distinct kernel geometries, while finite-shot robustness remains strongly task-and configuration-dependent.

title: Improving the Restore Performance of Fine-Grained Deduplication on Docker Container Image Storage
authors: Haoliang Tan, Wenhao Ou, Xiangyu Zou, Lisha Qin, Cai Deng, Zhaoquan Gu, Wen Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: The rapid growth of container images leads to heavy storage pressures on image registries. Fine-grained deduplication, such as at the file and chunk levels, is a promising technique for reducing the storage space in image registries, compared to Docker's native coarse-grained image layer deduplication. However, fine-grained deduplication often incurs significant image restoration latency due to fragmented I/Os, resulting in up to 8× restoration I/O slowdowns. Moreover, existing restore-optimized deduplication techniques are not tailored to the characteristics of the container image, resulting in low deduplication ratios of images. Consequently, restoring performance remains a critical barrier to the practical adoption of fine-grained deduplication in container image registries. To address this challenge, we propose MiDedup, a restore-friendly fine-grained deduplication approach tailored for container image registries. MiDedup is built on an underexplored observation: the redundancy across layers of images follows a non-uniform distribution. Based on this insight, MiDedup introduces three core techniques: ① Across-layer-aware reorganization, which reorganizes deduplicated data into a compact, sequential layout, significantly reduces fragmented I/Os during image restore. ② Popularity-aware rewriting, which selectively rewrites hot image layers to further improve the balance between deduplication ratio and restore performance. ③ Hybrid-granularity deduplication, which combines file-level and chunk-level deduplication to reduce metadata and fragmented data. Experiments on the real-world Docker image dataset show that MiDedup reduces the 50–80% restore I/O overhead of fine-grained deduplication, which is comparable to the restore performance of native layer-level deduplication, while achieving 1.2–7.3× higher deduplication ratios.

title: Miniaturized Wideband Filtering Antenna on Glass Substrate for mm-Wave AiP
authors: Sha Xu, Hongsheng Cai, Mankai Jiang, Qiongsen Wu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a miniaturized wideband millimeter-wave (mm-wave) filtering antenna with high out-of-band suppression, implemented on a glass substrate using through-glass via (TGV) technology. The antenna topology integrates a primary radiating patch with parasitic elements and a set of planar inverted-F antennas (PIFAs). The PIFA structures, enabled by metallized vias, are key to achieving both a wide impedance bandwidth and a sharp gain roll-off in the lower stopband. Concurrently, two sets of slots etched onto the main patch introduce two additional radiation nulls to ensure strong suppression in the upper stopband. The single antenna element maintains a compact footprint of 0.7 λ0 × 0.56 λ0 × 0.065 λ0 (@ 24 GHz). For experimental validation, a 1 4 linear array was fabricated and measured. The measured results demonstrate a 10-dB bandwidth of 21.8 - 30 GHz ( 31.7%) and a peak gain of 10.7 dBi. The three well-defined radiation nulls collectively provide over 13 dB of out-of-band suppression. The combination of wideband filtering performance, compact footprint, and a silicon-compatible glass-based platform make the proposed antenna an excellent candidate for highly integrated Antenna-in-Package (AiP) solutions for 5G and beyond mm-wave communication systems.

title: Event-based Facial Keypoint Alignment via Cross-Modal Fusion Attention and Self-Supervised Multi-Event Representation Learning
authors: Donghwa Kang, Junho Kim, Dongwoo Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Event cameras offer unique advantages for facial keypoint alignment under challenging conditions, such as low light and rapid motion, due to their high temporal resolution and robustness to varying illumination. However, existing RGB facial keypoint alignment methods do not perform well on event data, and training solely on event data often leads to suboptimal performance because of its limited spatial information. Moreover, the lack of comprehensive labeled event datasets further hinders progress in this area. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework based on cross-modal fusion attention (CMFA) and self-supervised multi-event representation learning (SSMER) for event-based facial keypoint alignment. Our framework employs CMFA to integrate corresponding RGB data, guiding the model to extract robust facial features from event input images. In parallel, SSMER enables effective feature learning from unlabeled event data, overcoming spatial limitations. Extensive experiments on our real‑event E‑SIE dataset and a synthetic‑event version of the public WFLW‑V benchmark show that our approach consistently surpasses state‑of‑the‑art methods across multiple evaluation metrics.

title: Towards Generalized and Explainable Face Anti-Spoofing via Decomposition–Fusion Networks
authors: Dhimas Arief Dharmawan, Anto Satriyo Nugroho, Seviko Attalarik Pramayudha Handoko
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: This paper presents DFNet, a decomposition–fusion network for generalized and interpretable face anti-spoofing. DFNet comprises two explicitly designed branches—a decomposition branch and a fusion branch—that jointly enhance robustness and interpretability in presentation attack detection. The decomposition branch extracts multi-level representations through a two-stage process, where coarse features and saliency maps are first computed to emphasize prominent regions, followed by the suppression of these regions to facilitate fine-grained feature extraction from less dominant areas. The fusion branch integrates the coarse and fine-grained streams using a residual architecture augmented with convolutional block attention modules, while a dynamic weighting mechanism adaptively balances their contributions during feature fusion. DFNet is extensively evaluated on multiple public benchmarks under both intra- and cross-dataset protocols, and its robustness is further assessed under strong Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) adversarial attacks. Experimental results demonstrate that DFNet achieves competitive or superior performance across diverse datasets and attack scenarios, while maintaining improved resilience to adversarial perturbations. Moreover, the modular, attention-driven design of DFNet provides saliency-based insights into the learned spoof traces, supporting its interpretability. Overall, this work high-lights the effectiveness of explicitly incorporating generalization, robustness, and interpretability into architectural design for real-world face anti-spoofing, with DFNet offering a concrete step toward this goal. For reproducibility, the source code is publicly available at https://github.com/dhimasarief/DFNet.

title: Proficient implementation of Resource Allocation in New Radio based Vertical Network Slicing
authors: Kalpana Naidu, Narasimha Rao Banavathu, Vanlin Sathya, Kota Nageswara Rao, Sunkaraboina Sreenu
abstract: This paper addresses the channel and power allocation (CAP) problem in a downlink vertical network slicing (VNS) system. In this downlink, a Terrestrial Base Station and an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) cooperatively serve multiple ground users. Besides, UAV exploits both licensed and unlicensed spectrum resources to enhance the network capacity. Thus, an optimization framework for CAP is developed to maximize the aggregate downlink throughput under interference and minimum-data rate constraints. In order to resolve CAP, conventional iterative algorithms incur high computational complexity. In contrast, the proposed analytical approach provides predominantly closed-form solutions for the CAP problem. Further, the proposed analysis establishes the uniqueness of the obtained solution by deriving closed-form expressions for the required number of channels (based on channel conditions). Moreover, simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed framework. Besides, simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves lower computational complexity, while maintaining performance comparable to that of iterative algorithms.

title: ELA-FPM: An Energy-Efficient LUT-based Approximate Floating-Point Multiplier for Neural Network Acceleration
authors: Mahla Salehi Sheikhali Kelayeh, Asma Naserirad, Sahand Divsalar, Omid Salehinia, Shaghayegh Vahdat
abstract: As artificial intelligence applications grow in practicality and complexity, efficient hardware implementation of these algorithms turns into a critical issue. Floating-point multiplication is one of the most fundamental, yet power-hungry, operations in modern artificial intelligence algorithms. This paper proposes a novel floating-point approximate multiplier, called ELA-FPM, which uses a combination of static segmentation of input operands and an energy-efficient lookup table-based method to significantly alleviate the overhead of mantissa multiplication, which is the most power-consuming operation in a floating-point multiplier. The superiority of ELA-FPM manifests itself as delay, power, and area improvements of up to 68.3%, 95.3%, and 92.1%, compared to an exact floating-point multiplier and improvements of up to 65.6%, 76%, and 71%, compared to other state-of-the-art approximate multipliers. Furthermore, ELA-FPM is tested on some of the most famous convolutional neural network models, demonstrating a maximum accuracy drop of 0.67% compared to a model that uses exact multipliers.

title: Active Coupling Control-Based Transient Frequency–Voltage Coordinated Support for Grid-Following Inverters
authors: Jingrong Yu, Linyi Ju, Yijun Wang, Yan Li, Jiaqi Yu
abstract: The modern power systems with high proportion of renewable energy features “weak frequency” and “weak voltage” characteristics, imposing stringent requirement on the transient frequency and voltage support of grid-following (GFL) inverters in renewable energy generation systems. Existing grid support methods for GFL inverters are unable to simultaneously address both rate of change of frequency (ROCOF) and rate of change of voltage (ROCOV) under grid disturbances such as voltage sags. To overcome this limitation, the proposed method investigates the coupling mechanism between transient frequency and voltage when the grid impedance is of resistive-inductive characteristics. On the basis of this coupling mechanism, a frequency–voltage coordinated support method is proposed by employ an active coupling gain between the active power and reactive power. Compared with the existing method, the proposed method employs the active coupling rather than decouple ideas to enable the joint optimization of transient rate of change of ROCOF and ROCOV, yielding better grid compatibility with wide variation range of short-circuit ratio (SCR) and resistance-to-inductance(X/R) ratio, and smaller extrema of ROCOF and ROCOV. Both simulation and experimental results verified the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method.

title: 2025 3rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Power Engineering (AIPE)
authors: N/A

title: LISK: A High-Performance In-Memory Learned Index for Variable-Length String Keys
authors: Zhaole Chu, Yigui Yuan, Junli Wang, Xiaoliang Wang, Peiquan Jin
abstract: Learned index has emerged as a new indexing technique that leverages machine learning to accelerate in-memory data processing. However, current learned indexes are primarily designed to index numeric keys and lack robust support for variable-length string keys. In this paper, we propose a novel in-memory learned index called LISK (<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</u>earned <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</u>ndex for <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u>tring <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">K</u>eys) to support string keys. The novelty of LISK is two-fold. First, we propose a trie-like structure to address the limitations of linear models in fitting string keys. Each trie node indexes 8-byte key slices, which are organized as learned sub-indexes or B+-trees. Second, we present a new structure for learned sub-indexes, namely TLS (<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</u>wo-layer <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</u>earned <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u>ubindex), which is tailored to handle the complex distribution of string keys. TLS utilizes three key designs to improve the overall performance: (1) a two-phase hybrid index construction, (2) a second-derivative-based data partitioning, and (3) a cachefriendly overflow node design. We conduct extensive experiments on five datasets and six workloads to compare LISK with seven existing indexes, including five trie-based indexes and the state-of-the-art learned index LITS. The experimental results show that LISK achieves an average 1.99× (up to 7.87×) higher throughput across the six workloads on real-world datasets. Specifically, compared with LITS, LISK achieves an average 1.42× (up to 1.91× ) higher throughput. The source code of LISK is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/suibianll/LISK/tree/master</uri>.

title: Identification of Critical Frequencies in Wideband Near-Field Spatial Scans
authors: Tomas Monopoli, Xinglong Wu, Cheng Yang, Christian Schuster, Sergio Amedeo Pignari, Johannes Wolf, Flavia Grassi
abstract: Identifying critical frequencies of a device under test (DUT) from its near-field scans is an essential step in integrating near-field scanning into electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) testing. Indeed, real electronic devices may exhibit radiated emissions at multiple frequencies, which are unknown a priori. To this end, three computational methods are introduced to identify the frequencies at which the DUT is radiating: the space-filled spectrogram analysis, the grey level co-occurrence matrix approach, and spatial fast Fourier transform approach. Although a single general solution is not provided, each method leverages different properties of the measurements to extract significant features and identify frequencies of interest. Therefore, the choice of which method to apply depends on the characteristics of the DUT and on the requirements of the specific measurement campaign. The proposed methods are evaluated on a common dataset, assessing their computational efficiency, sensitivity to patterns, and robustness against noise. Case studies demonstrate their successful application in real-world scenarios and their comparative analysis provides insights into selecting the most appropriate approach for a specific application.
